ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for San Giovannese pasta. FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for San Giovannese pasta with tomato-olive-caper sauce.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of San Giovannese pasta is approximately 300 g, comprising a full plate of cooked pasta dressed with cherry tomato, olive, caper, garlic, chilli, and Parmesan sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with tomato sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20420 and composite)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for San Giovannese pasta. Values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pasta (NDB 20420), canned tomatoes, black olives, capers, garlic, chilli, and Parmesan cheese in proportions consistent with a classic Italian tomato-olive-caper pasta sauce. Parmesan contribution raises protein, fat, and sodium. Olive and caper contributions raise sodium and fat. Per-100g values represent the plated dish including sauce.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for San Giovannese pasta. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for spaghetti with tomato sauce (GI ~40–50, mean ~45), which is the closest analogous dish. The addition of olives, capers, and Parmesan does not materially alter the glycemic response, which is dominated by the pasta matrix. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 20.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 27.7 g, based on 300 g serving size and 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from olives, capers, and Parmesan cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of San Giovannese pasta due to its elevated sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g), primarily contributed by salt-cured capers, brined black olives, and aged Parmesan; a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing more than half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from pasta carbohydrates, partially offset by low GI pasta matrix
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume San Giovannese pasta in controlled portions; the low GI of approximately 45 reflects the dense pasta matrix that slows glucose absorption, but the glycemic load per standard 300 g serving is approximately 27.7 g, which is moderate-to-high and warrants portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables or a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from Parmesan; beneficial monounsaturated fat from olive oil; high sodium from cured ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: San Giovannese pasta presents a mixed cardiovascular profile: olive oil contributes cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern, while the high sodium load from capers, olives, and Parmesan and the moderate saturated fat content from Parmesan cheese warrant moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: San Giovannese is a classic Italian pasta dish featuring a sauce of cherry tomatoes, black olives, capers, garlic, chilli, and Parmesan cheese, served over a long or short pasta such as spaghetti or penne. The dish originates from the southern Italian culinary tradition and shares flavour characteristics with pasta alla puttanesca, though it is distinguished by the use of Parmesan rather than anchovies as the primary umami-rich ingredient. The sauce is prepared by sautéing garlic and chilli in olive oil, adding halved cherry tomatoes and cooking until they collapse into a light, vibrant sauce, then folding in brined black olives and salt-packed capers rinsed to reduce salinity. Parmesan is grated over the finished dish. The result is a boldly flavoured, slightly spicy, savoury pasta with a balance of acidity from the tomatoes, brininess from the olives and capers, heat from the chilli, and umami depth from the Parmesan. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (approximately 148 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from both pasta and Parmesan, and a low-to-moderate glycemic index driven by the dense pasta matrix. Sodium content is elevated due to the cured and brined ingredients, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan or gluten-free. It contains gluten (pasta) and dairy (Parmesan). In Singapore, it is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore and represents a mid-range to premium casual dining option.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for San Giovannese pasta exists in any approved primary source (HPB, USDA, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS). Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pasta, canned tomatoes, black olives, capers, garlic, chilli, and Parmesan in proportions consistent with the described dish. GI is estimated from the closest analogous dish (spaghetti with tomato sauce) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: San Giovannese is a named pasta dish served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore (Boat Quay, Singapore) and is not a widely documented canonical Italian dish with standardised nutrient data in any approved database. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on USDA SR Legacy data for individual components. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for this dish type in the source database and is expected to be negligible. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g, which is confirmed zero for this olive-oil-based dish with no hydrogenated fats. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and all micronutrients marked -1 are not directly reported in the composite source and have been estimated where possible from component data; vitamin D specifically is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients in meaningful quantities. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database spaghetti with tomato sauce data. Sodium is elevated due to brined olives, salt-packed capers, and aged Parmesan. The dish is classified as Italian cuisine; multilingual local-name fields for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean dish and no established local-language names exist for this Italian restaurant menu item.
